title: Rethinking Joint Maximum Mean Discrepancy for Visual Domain Adaptation

reviews.summary: This paper revisits Joint Maximum Mean Discrepancy (JMMD) for domain adaptation and derives a concise, kernelized formulation that avoids explicit tensor products and enables optimization in subspace-learning frameworks via the Representer theorem. It further shows that several popular distribution-alignment distances (marginal, class-conditional, and weighted class-conditional MMDs) are special cases of JMMD with appropriately constructed label kernels, and analyzes why JMMD can harm discrimination by contrasting it with HSIC from a graph-embedding perspective. Motivated by this analysis, the paper proposes a combined loss, JMMD-HSIC, and demonstrates consistent albeit modest improvements over strong shallow baselines on several cross-domain benchmarks.
strengths: The concise JMMD derived via the Representer theorem replaces an infinite-dimensional projection with a finite coefficient matrix and removes explicit tensor products, yielding a practical formulation tr(B^T K^XX (K^YY ⊙ M^J) K^XX B) suitable for subspace learning.

The “uniformity” viewpoint is insightful: it shows that marginal, class-conditional, and weighted class-conditional distances are instances of JMMD through specific label kernels. This provides a unifying lens and suggests a principled path to designing problem-specific label kernels.

The graph-embedding interpretation that explains the discrimination degradation of JMMD is intuitive and compelling: it makes explicit how JMMD’s induced similarity weights oppose intra-class compactness within domains, whereas HSIC promotes it.
The proposed JMMD-HSIC combines global alignment (JMMD) with per-domain discriminability reinforcement (HSIC) in a unified, kernelized trace form with a simple closed-form gradient.
The method is integrated into three representative subspace-learning frameworks (JDA, SPL, OGL²P), and evaluated across four standard DA datasets using both handcrafted and deep features.

Ablation studies examine the effects of individual losses (JMMD vs HSIC vs combined), kernel choices for the label space (K¹/K²/K³/K⁴), metric-based diagnostics (distribution distance and feature-label dependence), and sensitivity to the balancing hyperparameter δ.
Qualitative visualizations (t-SNE) are consistent with the theoretical claims about the trade-off between alignment and discriminability.
The conceptual pipeline is clear: concise JMMD via Representer theorem, uniformity via label kernels, HSIC complement, and the combined JMMD-HSIC loss; figures reinforce the intuition.
The graph-embedding interpretation neatly connects distribution alignment terms to familiar Laplacian-based embedding objectives.
Making JMMD practically optimizable in subspace-learning pipelines is valuable for a large body of shallow DA methods and kernel approaches.
The unifying label-kernel perspective has the potential to guide principled extensions of JMMD to varied DA settings (e.g., class imbalance, conditional shift).
weaknesses: Key derivations (especially Eq. (2) from the Representer theorem, and the PSD proofs for K²/K³/K⁴) are deferred to the supplement; without a sketch, it is hard to verify the elimination of tensor products and the kernel validity in the main text.

The proposed HSIC label kernel K⁴ includes negative entries; while you state it is a reproducing kernel, this is nontrivial and requires more justification in the main text (e.g., factorization or construction that guarantees PSD).

The “graph Laplacian” analogy uses weight patterns that can be negative (for JMMD), which raises questions about the precise Laplacian construction and minimization properties without further constraints.

The study focuses on shallow/subspace-learning baselines and does not compare against modern joint-alignment methods implemented in deep architectures (e.g., JAN/JMMD, DeepJDOT, kernel Bures–Wasserstein joint alignment such as BJDA). This limits the assessment of significance for current practice where end-to-end deep DA dominates.
Improvements over baselines are generally modest (≈0.5–1.4% on average), with no confidence intervals or statistical tests to assess robustness; per-task variance is not reported.

The approach relies on target pseudo-labels for the joint term; the paper lacks a detailed protocol on how soft labels are obtained/updated, thresholds used, and how robustness to noisy pseudo-labels is ensured.
Computational aspects are not discussed: the K^XX Gram matrix scales as O(n^2) memory and O(n^3) time for some operations; guidance on scalability and runtime is missing.

Notational inconsistencies (e.g., using I for an all-ones matrix; ambiguity around Hadamard vs “dot-product” operator ⊙) and missing definitions (exact forms of M^J and M^H in the main text) hinder immediate reproducibility.
HSIC is typically maximized; the paper switches to a minimization with a specially crafted kernel—this deserves clearer exposition and intuition in the main text (beyond “advantages” listed).
Joint distribution alignment alternatives (e.g., kernel Bures/OT-based BJDA and related RKHS joint metrics) are not discussed or compared.

Recent DA approaches that explicitly balance transferability/discriminability (beyond BSP) and dependence-based criteria could be cited and contrasted more thoroughly, especially methods that also analyze alignment-induced discrimination loss.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide a concise proof sketch in the main paper for Eq. (2) (the Representer-based elimination of the tensor product and the derivation of the trace form with K^XX, K^YY, and M^J), including key assumptions?
2. How do you guarantee that K⁴ is positive semidefinite despite negative off-diagonal entries? A constructive factorization or eigenvalue argument in the main text would be valuable.
3. What are the exact definitions of M^J and M^H used in your experiments (including centering, within/between-domain blocks, and any normalization)? Please include them in the main paper.
4. How are target pseudo-labels obtained and updated for K^YY in practice within SPL and OGL²P (e.g., hard vs soft, confidence thresholds, update frequency)? How sensitive are results to pseudo-label noise?
5. What is the computational/memory footprint of your losses (e.g., complexity in n and d), and how do they scale to larger datasets? Any approximations (e.g., Nyström, mini-batch estimates) that preserve performance?
6. Can you report standard deviations across multiple runs and/or statistical tests for Tables 1–2 to quantify the reliability of the observed gains?
7. How does JMMD-HSIC compare empirically to representative deep joint-alignment baselines (JAN/JMMD, DeepJDOT, BJDA) when integrated into a simple deep adaptor (e.g., two FC layers on ResNet features)?
8. In highly imbalanced settings, does K³ (weighted class-conditional) make a measurable difference over K²? Can you provide experiments on a deliberately imbalanced split to validate Theorem 1’s practical utility?
reviews.assessment: The paper presents a thoughtful and mostly well-executed re-examination of JMMD: a concise RKHS formulation usable in subspace-learning, a useful unifying kernel view of existing distances, and an intuitive graph-embedding analysis that clarifies why JMMD can erode discriminability. The proposed JMMD-HSIC is principled, easy to optimize, and yields consistent, if modest, gains across several datasets and baselines. However, critical theoretical details are deferred to the supplement; the empirical evaluation does not yet position the method against contemporary joint-alignment approaches in deep settings; and improvements are relatively small without statistical reporting. Overall, the work is solid and potentially useful to the kernel/subspace-learning DA community, but in its current form falls short of the bar for NeurIPS due to incomplete theoretical exposition in the main text and limited empirical breadth. Strengthening the derivations in the paper body, adding deep-method comparisons, providing robustness analyses to pseudo-label noise, and reporting variance would significantly bolster the case.
